title: MKT10009 Lecture 2 Part 4
summary: MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 2Part 4 - Business markets and business buyer behaviour. Chapter 5 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing, 8th edition Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell Learning Objectives for Lecture
content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 2Part 4  - Business markets and business buyer behaviour.
Chapter 5 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Learning Objectives for Lecture 2 – Part 4
5.4 Define the business market and identify the main factors that influence business buyer behaviour.
5.5 List and define the steps in the business buying decision process.

Part 4  - Business markets and business buyer behaviour

Business markets and business buyer behaviour (1 of 7)
Business buyer behaviour is the buying behaviour of the organisations that buy goods and services for use in the production of other products and services or to resell or rent them to others at a profit.
Business-to-business (B2B) marketers must:
do their best to understand business markets and business buyer behaviour
build profitable relationships with business customers by creating superior customer value.
Q: ‘In one way or another, most large companies sell to other organisations.’ Can you give some examples?

Business markets and business buyer behaviour (2 of 7)
Business markets
The business market is huge. In fact, business markets involve far more dollars and items than do consumer markets.
Business marketing includes:
resellers (the reseller market)
manufacturers (the industrial market)
institutional and government markets.

Business markets and business buyer behaviour (3 of 7)
Business markets (cont.)
Market structure and demand
Business markets normally have far fewer but far larger buyers.
Business demand is derived demand – it ultimately comes from (derives from) the demand for consumer goods.
Business markets have both:
inelastic demand, i.e. total demand is not affected much by price changes, especially in the short run
fluctuating demand, i.e. demand for business goods and services tends to change more, and more quickly, than the demand for consumer goods.

Figure 5.7
A model of business buyer behaviour

Business markets and business buyer behaviour (5 of 7)
Business markets (cont.)
Main types of buying situations
Straight rebuy is a business buying situation in which the buyer routinely reorders something without any modifications.
Modified rebuy is a business buying situation in which the buyer wants to modify product specifications, prices, terms or suppliers.
New-task situation is a business buying situation in which the buyer purchases a product or service for the first time.
Systems selling (solutions selling) refers to buying a packaged solution to a problem from a single seller, thus avoiding all the separate decisions involved in a complex buying situation.

Business markets and business buyer behaviour (6 of 7)
Business markets (cont.)
Participants in the business buying process
The buying centre is all the individuals and units that play a role in the business purchase decision-making process.
The main influences on business buyers
There are various groups of influences on business buyers:
environmental
organisational
interpersonal
individual.

Figure 5.8
Major influences on business buyer behaviour

The business buying process (1 of 2)
There are eight stages of the business buying process.
Buyers who face a new-task buying situation usually go through all stages of the business buying process.
Buyers making modified or straight rebuys may skip some of the stages.
Different buying centre participants may be involved at different stages of the process.
Buyers do not always follow the steps in the same order, and they may add other steps or repeat certain stages of the process.

The business buying process (2 of 2)
Figure 5.9
Stages of the business buying process

E-procurement: Buying on the internet (1 of 2)
E-procurement refers to purchasing through electronic connections between buyers and sellers – usually online.
Companies can do e-procurement by:
conducting reverse auctions
engaging in online trading exchanges
setting up their own company buying sites
creating extranet links with key suppliers or partners.
Q: What is a reverse auction?

E-procurement: Buying on the internet (2 of 2)
B2B e-procurement yields many benefits. A few of these are:
shaves transaction costs and results in more efficient purchasing for both buyers and suppliers
reduces the time between order and delivery
eliminates the paperwork associated with traditional requisition and ordering procedures.
Q: What other benefits does e-procurement have? What are some of the problems?

Business-to-business digital and social media marketing
In response to customers’ rapid shift towards online buying, today’s B2B marketers use a wide range of digital and social media marketing approaches.
These approaches include:
websites and blogs
mobile apps
e-newsletters and proprietary online networks
social media, such as Facebook, LinkedIn, YouTube, Instagram, Twitter.
Digital and social media allow B2B marketers to engage customers and manage customer relationships anywhere, any time.

Business-to-business digital and social media marketing
In response to customers’ rapid shift towards online buying, today’s B2B marketers use a wide range of digital and social media marketing approaches.
These approaches include:
websites and blogs
mobile apps
e-newsletters and proprietary online networks
social media, such as Facebook, LinkedIn, YouTube, Instagram, Twitter.
Digital and social media allow B2B marketers to engage customers and manage customer relationships anywhere, any time.

5.4 Define the business market and identify the main factors that influence business buyer behaviour.
